key_insight: Task-specific LoRA adapters in continual fine-tuning are often redundant: their low-rank subspaces overlap enough that earlier adapters can sometimes represent later tasks.
engineering_relevance: For continual learning workflows, selective adapter reuse can cut memory and training overhead while preserving accuracy, making parameter-efficient adaptation more practical at scale.
method: Analyze overlap between LoRA subspaces learned on sequential tasks.
Introduce LiteLoRA, a train-time gating mechanism that chooses between recruiting a new adapter or reusing existing ones.
Reduce active adapters by selectively learning only when reuse is insufficient.
evidence_signal: Across standard continual learning benchmarks, LiteLoRA reduces the number of active adapters by 20–70% while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance.
watch_item: Performance depends on the gating policy and task similarity, so weak reuse decisions could still hurt adaptation on highly dissimilar tasks.
paper_title: When One Adapter Speaks for Many: Discovering Low-Rank Redundancy in Continual Fine-Tuning
